title: What architectural factors influence vector search latency at scale, beyond the ANN algorithm itself?
prompt:
What architectural factors influence vector search latency at scale, beyond the ANN algorithm itself?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
End-to-end RAG latency is governed by embedding inference, index memory residency, and payload fetch round-trips — not just the ANN search step itself. Embedding API latency: query embedding generation, typically 50-150ms, frequently dominates total RAG latency over the ANN search that follows it. Index placement: in-memory HNSW responds in under 10ms, while disk-backed SSD search adds real NVMe read latency on top. Pre vs post filtering: metadata post-filtering (search first, then filter) ruins recall because you may filter away most of the top-k results after the fact — single-stage pre-filtering integrated into the HNSW traversal maintains both speed and recall. Payload hydration: store chunk text directly alongside the vector, or batch-fetch chunk content from SQL, to prevent an N+1 round-trip pattern where each retrieved chunk ID triggers a separate document lookup.

**Takeaway:** Before optimizing the ANN index itself, check whether embedding generation, disk-backed index placement, post-filtering recall loss, or N+1 payload fetches are the actual latency source — the search algorithm is often not the bottleneck.
hint_notes: 4 latency sources beyond ANN search itself: embedding API call time, in-memory vs disk-backed index, post- vs pre-filtering (recall/speed tradeoff), and N+1 payload hydration round-trips.
